context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi — I’m working on the City Case Study Spider Diagram, and I’m kind of unsure how to start organizing it. I think it has something to do with the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation, but I’m not fully clear on what kind of info I’m supposed to put in each part.
exchanges.tutor: Good starting point — understanding the 5 Pathways is definitely the key to organizing the diagram. Before we dig into the structure, tell me: what do you already know about the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from your course materials? Even a rough idea is fine.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the very beginning of the assignment and needs orientation without being given the structure outright. I should check what they already know about the 5 Pathways before guiding them further, using a Socratic opening question. This keeps scaffolding minimal and puts the thinking on the student.

exchanges.student: Honestly, only vaguely. I remember they’re like different areas cities can act in for climate change, but I mix them up and I’m not sure if they’re sectors, strategies, or something else.
exchanges.tutor: That's a reasonable starting intuition. Before going further, I'd strongly encourage you to go back to your course materials for Week 1 or whichever module introduced the 5 Pathways — they should be defined there. Once you have them in front of you, come back and we can work through how they map onto your case study city. Which city did you choose, by the way?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a vague but partially correct intuition — the Pathways are indeed areas of action, not just sectors. Rather than explaining the Pathways to them, I should redirect them to their course materials, since this is foundational knowledge they need to retrieve themselves before they can complete the assignment meaningfully. Giving them even a partial list would undermine the Socratic approach.

exchanges.student: I picked Rotterdam. I can check the course page, but before I do, can you help me understand what I should be looking for when I find the 5 pathways so I don’t just copy terms without getting them?
exchanges.tutor: Great question, and Rotterdam is a strong choice. When you read about each Pathway, ask yourself: what kind of climate action does this cover, and can I think of something Rotterdam is actually doing that fits here? That way you're connecting the concept to a real example right away, rather than treating it as an abstract label. Go check the materials and come back with what you find!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a meta-learning question, which is legitimate and useful. I can briefly guide them on how to engage meaningfully with the Pathways without revealing what the Pathways are. This keeps them active in the learning process and prepares them to apply the concepts rather than just copy them.

exchanges.student: Okay, I’ll do that. I’m guessing I should probably write down each pathway and then note one Rotterdam example under each, but I’m not sure yet if that’s too simple.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct is pointing in the right direction. But before assuming, have you looked closely at the Spider Diagram table template described in the assignment? That structure itself will tell you what kind and how much information is expected per Pathway. What does the template ask for?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making a reasonable guess about the structure but is second-guessing themselves. Rather than validating or correcting their assumption directly, I should prompt them to look at the assignment description more carefully — specifically the table/diagram structure — so they can answer their own question. The assignment asks them to map climate action across the 5 Pathways, so their instinct is directionally right but they need to discover the full scope themselves.

exchanges.student: I haven’t looked at the template closely yet, which is probably why I feel lost. I’ll check that too, but if the table has more than just “pathway + example,” I may need help figuring out what counts as enough detail.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — the template is your roadmap. Go look at both the 5 Pathways in your course materials and the table template, then come back with what you find. We can work through the detail question once you have something concrete in front of you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is being proactive and self-aware, which is good. I should encourage them to look at both the course materials and the template before coming back with questions, so our next exchange can be more productive and grounded in what they've actually found. No scaffolding needed here — just a clear next step.

exchanges.student: Got it. I’ll pull up both and come back with the pathway names and the table headings, because I think I need that before I can make a decent start.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect plan. See you when you're back with that information — that's exactly the right starting point.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has a clear and productive plan. No further guidance is needed at this point — I should affirm their approach briefly and wait for them to return with the materials before continuing.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I’ll be back once I’ve got it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is stepping away to gather materials. A brief, encouraging sign-off is appropriate. Nothing more is needed.
The student is simply acknowledging. No academic content to address. A very brief response is appropriate to keep the conversation open without adding unnecessary content.

The student is still just signing off with small talk. No academic content to engage with. I should keep it very brief and wait for them to return with their materials rather than prolonging idle chat.
Pure social exchange, no academic content. One word response is sufficient.
